stem: For construction cost estimation, the base rate for a bridge project depends on which reference class is used: Reference Class A (all infrastructure projects nationally, n=500): base rate = 15%. Reference Class B (similar-scale bridge projects, n=100): base rate = 28%. Reference Class C (projects by this contractor in this region, n=15): base rate = 43%. Each reference class has a different level of specificity and sample size. What is the appropriate base rate to use, and how should the choice of reference class affect the uncertainty in the prediction?

ground_truth.answer: The reference class problem: different classes give rates of 15%, 28%, 43%. No single class is objectively 'correct'. Combined interval spanning all classes: [0.121, 0.668]. Weighted point estimate (specificity-weighted): 0.327.

solution_steps: Class A (all infrastructure projects nationally): rate=0.15, n=500, CI=[0.121, 0.184]. Large sample but least specific.
Class B (similar-scale bridge projects): rate=0.28, n=100, CI=[0.201, 0.375]. Moderate specificity and sample size.
Class C (projects by this contractor in this region): rate=0.43, n=15, CI=[0.22, 0.668]. Most specific but smallest sample (widest CI).
The reference class problem has no definitive solution — the 'correct' base rate depends on which class membership is considered most relevant.
Full uncertainty range spanning all reference classes: [0.121, 0.668].
common_failure_modes: Choosing one reference class without acknowledging the others
Using only the most specific class (Class C) despite its small sample size
Using only the broadest class (Class A) despite its low relevance
Not recognizing that the choice of reference class is itself a source of fundamental uncertainty
